main_summary.summary: Worth W. Long, born Worth Westmoreland Long Jr. on January 15, 1936 at Duke Hospital in Durham, North Carolina, was interviewed in Jackson, Mississippi on December 6, 2015 by Emilye Crosby for the Civil Rights History Project. The available transcript covers Long's family origins and early Durham upbringing. His father, Rev. William Worth Long Sr., was a presiding elder of the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church (AMEZ) who oversaw at least ten churches across the Durham district, traveling as a circuit rider and running quarterly conferences. His mother, Gertrude Watson Long, managed the household and the church's administrative work; she had previously worked at Liggett & Myers Tobacco Co and as a domestic for the Duke family. Long describes growing up on Formosa Avenue near North Carolina Central University, surrounded by neighbors including Henry McKinley Michaux, Dr. Albert L. Turner, and a piano studio run by C.C. Spaulding's daughter. He recalls riding his bicycle five miles to the family farm to milk cows and feed hogs, throwing newspapers at 5:30 a.m., and observing his father's hospitality to visitors including Floyd B. McKissick, Bishop Spottswood, and NAACP officials such as Alex Barnes. The transcript ends mid-sentence at 00:45:59 before reaching Long's SNCC or folklorist work.
key_themes: Family economy of farm and parsonage
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview documents the institutional ecology of mid-twentieth-century Black Durham, North Carolina, the intersection of the AME Zion Church's circuit-rider ministry, North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company, North Carolina Central University, and the Hayti commercial district. Long's account names specific figures (Floyd B. McKissick, Henry McKinley Michaux, C.C. Spaulding, Bishop Spottswood) and locates them within the residential and congregational networks that produced a generation of civil rights organizers.

The transcript also preserves first-person testimony about North Carolina's separate-but-equal evasions, including the state's decision to build a Black law school at North Carolina Central University rather than admit Floyd McKissick to the University of North Carolina, and the practice of paying Black teachers to pursue graduate degrees at Columbia and the University of Chicago rather than integrate state universities. Because the SRT is truncated at approximately 46 minutes mid-sentence, the available record covers only Long's early life and does not reach his SNCC fieldwork or his subsequent folklorist career.

chapters.title: Born at Duke Hospital, 1936
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in Jackson, Mississippi, on December 6, 2015, Worth W. Long opens by recounting his birth as Worth Westmoreland Long Jr. at Duke Hospital in Durham, North Carolina, on January 15, 1936, to Gertrude Watson Long and the Reverend William Worth Long Sr., an AME Zion minister born in Newell, North Carolina.

chapters.title: The Presiding Elder and His Administrator
chapters.summary: Long describes his father as a presiding elder of the AME Zion Church responsible for at least ten churches, riding circuit to hold quarterly conferences that handled general claims, mortgages, membership, and missionary funding for Africa, while his mother served not as a secretary but as an administrator managing the finances of both the home and the broad church network.

chapters.title: A Row to Talk Across
chapters.topic: The Farm and the Milk Route
chapters.summary: He recalls the family farm five miles from Durham that supplied their milk and garden food, his father driving out daily by jeep, and his own weekend chore of pedaling a bicycle out to milk the cow, feed the two hogs and the mule, and carry the milk carefully home, treasuring the rare unhurried talk when he and his formal, folk-spoken country-preacher father worked adjoining rows.

chapters.title: The Broom That Sealed the Deal
chapters.summary: Long traces his father's African lineage and first marriage into a free Negro family of Chatham County, recounted against Carter G. Woodson's census work, his year at Hood Theological Seminary near Livingstone College, and the arrival at his first church, Mitchell's Chapel AME Zion in Pittsboro, where the mother-in-law swept the dirt-floored parsonage with a homemade straw broom and sealed his thirty-year tenure on the district.

chapters.title: Gertrude's Table
chapters.summary: He describes his father's marriage to Gertrude Watson, a widow with three daughters whose settlement let her buy a Durham duplex, her work cleaning at Liggett and Myers and cooking for part of the Duke family, and the AME Zion officials she entertained, including Bishop Spottswood and her stepbrother Bishop E.B. Watson, who once gave young Worth a handmade gold palm clip that ruined his best tie.

chapters.title: They Had a Root Consciousness
chapters.summary: Long recalls NAACP figures such as Alex Barnes, who headed the AME Zion paper, coming to sit at Gertrude's table while he listened from the living room to talk of the church's business and the plight of Africans and the poor of the world, a household carrying at once a world consciousness and a deep African and root consciousness drawn from both parents' rural backgrounds.

chapters.title: The High-Class Neighborhood Came Up
chapters.summary: He explains that the family's lumber house, inherited near the planned North Carolina Central University, was soon surrounded on Formosa Avenue by the brick-and-stone homes of an arriving Black professional class: insurance men like Henry McKinley Michaux, a future president of North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company, and the piano studio of a daughter of C.C. Spaulding, so that as Long puts it the high-class neighborhood came up around them.

chapters.title: A Different World on the Block
chapters.summary: Long contrasts his under-heated, modest household with neighbors who sent their children to Palmer Memorial Institute and Camp Atwater in Massachusetts, situating it in Durham's standing as a financial center for Black America and crediting a women-founded business school that trained the competent accountants and typists North Carolina Mutual needed.

chapters.title: Can We Get Somebody to Fly All Guy
chapters.topic: The Manure Wagon and Paper Route
chapters.summary: He tells of dreading a daytime manure haul through the wealthy block and so driving the mule wagon in before dawn, only to be spotted and teased from the neighbors' porches, and of the paper route he ran from age five-thirty in the morning, collecting on weekends and absorbing the tidbits of folk advice and success ethic that customers pressed on him at every stop.

chapters.title: You Sponsor Their Education
chapters.summary: Long catalogs the self-sustaining Black district of Hayti, its Rialto and Booker T theaters, Bull City Drug Store, hotel and printing company, and describes his parents practicing community uplift by housing as many as eight college women who were required to live with a certified family when the girls' dormitory filled, each one adding to the counsel and encouragement of the household.

chapters.title: Wait a Minute, Seats in the Balcony
chapters.summary: He recounts his next-door neighbor Doc Holmes, a language professor who taught him to listen and pronounce German and French through Christmas carols and who, as head of the Lyceum program at North Carolina Central, slipped him into ballgames and then into balcony seats to hear Marian Anderson, Paul Robeson, and the pianist Philippa Schuyler, opening a whole new realm to him.

chapters.part: The Color Line and a Local Hero
chapters.title: They Built a Law School Instead
chapters.topic: McKissick and the UNC Color Line
chapters.summary: After a brief camera break, Long introduces Floyd B. McKissick, the young attorney who taught him civics, and recalls that rather than admit a Black man, the University of North Carolina built an entire law program at North Carolina Central, and that his Hillside High School teachers held master's degrees the state had subsidized at Chicago or Columbia rather than seat them in white universities, an absurdity of the system.

chapters.part: The Color Line and a Local Hero
chapters.title: Should I Fill It Up, Preach It?
chapters.summary: He describes riding with his minister father to the ten churches and a wartime gas stop near Chatham County where a filling-station boy, unsure how to address the well-dressed Black man and unwilling to say sir, settled on calling him preach it, an exchange Long reads as a human relationship built at a peer level and a lesson that self-image and change come only through engagement.

chapters.part: The Color Line and a Local Hero
chapters.title: A Chapter in Every Church
chapters.topic: McKissick, the NAACP, and the Church as Center
chapters.summary: Long returns to Floyd McKissick as the family lawyer and local hero with early nationalist ideas who would become national chairman of CORE, and to his father's insistence on an NAACP chapter in every church of the large Durham district, framing a vision in which the AME Zion church was never removed from the community but a center sponsoring education, economic development, and self-sufficiency, even buying and reselling mortgaged land.

loc_video.caption: Worth W. Long oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Jackson, Mississippi, 2015 December 06
